one_line_takeaway: In a 220,000-evaluation benchmark, the system prompt of an LLM email-triage agent is shown to be as consequential a security variable as model choice: signal-based prompt optimization can push phishing recall to 93.7% at 3.8% FPR, but the same domain-matching heuristic that enables this is provably and adversarially exploitable -- collapsing recall by up to 68.6 percentage points when an attacker simply registers matching sender/URL infrastructure, a gap the authors argue prompt engineering alone cannot close.
key_finding: System-prompt persona alone swings a single model's phishing bypass rate from under 1% to 97% (GPT-4o-mini), and this within-model variance often exceeds between-model variance at fixed configurations -- the six core personas span 7% to 55% average bypass rate with no configuration achieving both low bypass and low FPR (Safetility <=0.5% for all six core strategies). Signal-based prompt engineering (sender_url_match/trap_sender_match) achieves up to 93.7% recall at 3.8% FPR (GPT-4o-mini) and transfers cross-model without modification, but this optimization is adversarially brittle: infrastructure phishing (attacker-registered domain used as both sender and URL) collapses sender_url_match recall from 84.6% (commodity) to 43.1% (infra), and per-model losses reach -68.6 pp (GPT-4o-mini: 98.7%->30.1%) and -68.1 pp (Gemini 3 Flash). Response-trace analysis shows 98% of verbose bypass responses explicitly cite sender-URL domain consistency as evidence of legitimacy -- the models are executing the prompt's instruction faithfully, not failing to reason. A mitigation strategy (infra_aware) that grants override permission recovers infra recall for most models but at FPR costs varying by more than an order of magnitude across models (Gemini 2.5 Flash: +2.8 pp FPR cost vs Claude Haiku: +67.6 pp FPR), motivating a 'pre-deployment disposition' framework (calibrated / safety-amplifying / self-sufficient models). A targeted adversarial validation on 10 hard real-world infrastructure-phishing samples shows infra_aware still lets 90% (GPT-4o-mini), 90% (Gemini 2.5 Flash), and 70% (Grok 4.1) of attacks through, because the override's checks (domain reputation, sensitive-action detection, context plausibility) are content-based judgments the model cannot ground in facts (e.g. no WHOIS/domain-age data) and which the attacker fully controls. The paper also reports a counter-intuitive 'Instruction Specificity Paradox': narrowing instructions to a specific signal degrades already-strong detectors (Claude Haiku 4.5 recall drops 94.6%->75.4%, -19.2 pp) even though weak-baseline models (e.g. GPT-4o-mini) gain +38 to +61 pp.
core_contribution: The paper introduces PhishNChips, a 220,000-evaluation benchmark (11 LLMs x 10 system-prompt strategies x 2,000 emails) that studies how system-prompt configuration of autonomous LLM email agents shapes phishing-detection security outcomes. The paper's own contribution (as distinct from cited related work) is: (1) empirically showing that system-prompt persona is a first-order security variable whose within-model effect (e.g. GPT-4o-mini's bypass rate swinging from <1% to 97%) often exceeds between-model variance; (2) a 2x2 factorial experiment on two Gemini models isolating system-prompt vs user-prompt main effects; (3) three rounds of iterative signal-based prompt engineering (sender_url_match, trap_sender_match, infra_aware) that push several models to high recall/low FPR and generalize cross-model without modification; (4) discovery and characterization of an 'Instruction Specificity Paradox' where narrow signal-based instructions degrade already-strong detectors by up to 19.2 pp; (5) discovery of 'infrastructure phishing' (signal inversion / domain-matched attacker infrastructure) as an adversarial attack that collapses the optimized defenses by up to ~46-69 pp depending on model, plus response-trace analysis showing 98% of verbose bypass reasoning explicitly cites domain-match as evidence of legitimacy; (6) a targeted adversarial validation on 10 especially hard real infrastructure-phishing samples from live feeds showing the best mitigated prompt (infra_aware) still lets attacks through in 70-90% of cases on three Tier-1 models; and (7) a new deployability-aware composite metric, Safetility, that combines recall and FPR with a steep penalty above an operational FPR threshold (tau=0.10), showing only 4 of 110 tested (model, strategy) configurations are deployment-attractive.
threat_model: An autonomous LLM email agent (no human in the loop) that receives an inbound email and renders a binary verdict -- proceed (prediction=1) or block (prediction=0) -- on whether to allow embedded links/attachments through. The attacker's objective is to craft a phishing email (real malicious URLs drawn from PhishTank/OpenPhish Tier-1 feeds, LLM-generated body text) that induces the agent to proceed, leading to credential theft, malware delivery, or session hijacking. Two variants are studied: (a) commodity phishing, where sender and URL domains mismatch, and (b) 'infrastructure phishing'/signal inversion, where the attacker registers a single domain (~$10/yr) and uses it as both the sender address and the URL host, defeating sender-URL-consistency heuristics by construction. The paper explicitly distinguishes this threat model from prompt-injection/jailbreak threat models (e.g. AgentDojo, JailbreakBench): the adversary here crafts standard social-engineering phishing content aimed at deceiving the model/human the way it deceives human recipients, not adversarial inputs engineered to hijack the model's instruction-following.
limitations: The paper's own stated caveats: (1) The 2,000-email corpus is entirely synthetic (LLM-generated bodies), though real malicious URLs from Tier-1 feeds are used and cross-model consensus (98.7%) is offered as validation; (2) the benchmark's legitimate corpus is heavily domain-matched (98.4%), which the authors explicitly flag as inflating apparent low FPR for domain-matching strategies -- on an auxiliary 100-email cross-domain set, GPT-4o-mini's FPR rose from 3.8% (benchmark) to 72% and Grok 4.1's from 1.1% to 89% (19x and 81x inflation), so benchmark FPR figures are explicitly non-generalizable to production traffic with routine cross-domain links; (3) infrastructure-phishing sample size is small (n=73), giving per-model confidence intervals of roughly +/-10-12 pp, so individual-model collapse magnitudes should be read with that uncertainty even though aggregate patterns are called robust; (4) response-trace evidence rests on only the 61% of bypass responses that were verbose enough to parse (39% were terse, e.g. a bare '1'); (5) the models tested deliberately exclude 'reasoning-heavy'/pro-tier variants (o1, Claude Opus, Gemini Pro) as unrealistic for cost-sensitive triage deployment, so findings may not transfer to those models; (6) prompt strategies were developed/iterated on the Gemini family and then transferred to other models, so the specific numeric optima are Gemini-tuned even though the paper demonstrates cross-model generalization; (7) the paper itself concludes there is a ceiling on prompt-only defenses against infrastructure phishing because the required grounding (domain age, WHOIS, threat intel) is not available to a model reasoning over email text alone -- i.e., the authors argue their own approach cannot fully solve the problem it identifies without tool augmentation.

datasets_used: 2,000-email synthetic corpus (1,000 phishing / 1,000 legitimate) built by the authors
Real malicious URLs sourced from PhishTank and OpenPhish (Tier 1 human-verified phishing feeds)
73-sample infrastructure-phishing subset (domain-matched sender+URL), carved from the 1,000 phishing emails
Auxiliary 100-email synthetic cross-domain legitimate set (sender/URL domain intentionally mismatched)
10 real, live-feed infrastructure-phishing samples used for targeted adversarial validation (bypassed infra_aware on all 3 Tier-1 models)
quantitative_results: 220,000 total evaluations = 11 models x 10 strategies x 2,000 emails
GPT-4o-mini bypass rate: <1% (security-first persona) to 97% (efficiency-focused persona)
Core 6-strategy averages: security_first 7% bypass/57% FPR; balanced 30%/30%; baseline 43%/23%; trust_context 37%/32%; helpful 40%/36%; efficiency_first 55%/26% (Safetility <=0.5% for all six)
Factorial experiment (Gemini 3 Flash / Gemini 2.5 Flash): system-prompt main effect +32.0pp / +37.4pp recall; user-prompt main effect +0.9pp / +10.6pp; interaction -27.4pp / -30.7pp
Signal-based optimization best result: GPT-4o-mini 93.7% recall at 3.8% FPR under sender_url_match-family strategy (Safetility 87.1%)
Cross-model optimized gains: weak-baseline models +38 to +61pp (e.g., GPT-4o-mini +60.9pp, DeepSeek +47.8pp, Mistral +38.3pp)
Instruction Specificity Paradox: Claude Haiku 4.5 94.6%->75.4% recall (-19.2pp); Claude Sonnet 4.5 96.9%->90.2% (-6.7pp); GPT-5.2 94.7%->85.0% (-9.7pp)
Cross-domain FPR inflation on auxiliary set: GPT-4o-mini 3.8%->72% (19x), Grok 4.1 1.1%->89% (81x)

Commodity vs infrastructure recall by strategy: sender_url_match 84.6%->43.1% (-41.5pp); trap_sender_match 92.4%->46.5% (-46.0pp); trap_aggressive 98.6%->87.9% (-10.7pp); baseline 54.9%->81.2% (+26.3pp); security_first 93.1%->96.6% (+3.5pp)

Per-model infrastructure collapse under sender_url_match: Qwen3 -2.6pp (not significant), DeepSeek v3.2 -14.3pp, Claude Haiku 4.5 -13.4pp (resistant); Claude Sonnet 4.5 -26.4pp, GPT-5.2 -40.0pp, Mistral Small -43.1pp, GPT-4o-mini -68.6pp, Gemini 3 Flash -68.1pp, Llama 4 Scout -63.2pp, Grok 4.1 Fast -58.2pp, Gemini 2.5 Flash -58.5pp

Response-trace analysis: 457 total bypass responses on infra phishing under sender_url_match; 281 (61%) verbose; 98% of verbose responses cite domain consistency as evidence of legitimacy (65% domain consistency cited, 64% explicit 'domain match' language, 43% 'match'/'matches', 38% 'no red flags', 27% 'no mismatch', 19% professional tone)
infra_aware recovery: FPR cost range +2.8pp (Gemini 2.5 Flash) to +67.6pp (Claude Haiku 4.5) for infra recall gains; GPT-4o-mini efficiency ratio 10.6 (46.6pp infra-recall gain / 4.4pp FPR cost)
Adversarial validation on 10 hard real infra-phishing samples under infra_aware: GPT-4o-mini bypassed 9/10 (90%), Gemini 2.5 Flash bypassed 9/10 (90%), Grok 4.1 bypassed 7/10 (70%)

Safetility composite: only 4 of 110 (model, strategy) configurations rated deployment-attractive under the benchmark distribution; Grok 4.1 under trap_aggressive: 96.7% recall/5.0% FPR/90.7% Safetility (highest); DeepSeek under trap_aggressive: 99.8% recall/89.7% FPR/~0.00% Safetility; GPT-5.2 under balanced: 81.5% recall/22.2% FPR/1.2% Safetility
Instruction compliance (parseable binary output) ranged 79.8% (Llama 4 Scout) to 100% (Qwen3 235B, Claude Haiku 4.5, GPT-5.2)
baselines_compared: Six core (non-optimized) system-prompt personas: baseline, security_first, balanced, efficiency_first, helpful, trust_context -- used as the reference risk spectrum against which signal-based optimized strategies are compared
sender_url_match strategy used as the baseline against which infra_aware's recovery is measured
Commodity phishing recall used as the baseline against which infrastructure-phishing recall collapse is measured, per strategy and per model

Human phishing susceptibility rates from cited external studies (Verizon 2024; Braun et al. 2025: ~9-20% click rates; Rozema and Davis 2025: no statistically significant training effect, p=0.450) are referenced in the introduction for motivation/context, not as an experimental baseline the paper directly benchmarks against
